name: Strigoi
description: The Strigoi is a troubled spirit in Romanian folklore, said to have risen from the grave. These beings are often described as restless souls of people who died prematurely, unjustly, or lived lives of sin. They are believed to possess the power to transform into animals, such as wolves, bats, or cats, and to become invisible. Strigoi are known for their insatiable thirst for blood, which they obtain by preying on the living, causing sickness, and even death. They can also control the weather, cast spells, and possess living beings. Their origins are often tied to individuals with certain physical characteristics, such as a caul at birth, or those who died unbaptized or by suicide. The Strigoi is a powerful and malevolent creature, deeply ingrained in Romanian superstitions and folklore, inspiring fear and caution in rural communities.
subtitle: A Romanian undead spirit.
appearance: Strigoi are said to appear as pale, gaunt figures, often with red eyes and sharp teeth. They can also shapeshift into animals like wolves, bats, or cats.
habitat: Cemeteries, abandoned places, and the homes of their former lives.
behavior: Strigoi are primarily nocturnal creatures driven by a hunger for blood. They are known to attack livestock and humans, spreading disease and misfortune. They are also said to be envious of the living and seek to torment them.
weaknesses: Stakes made of hawthorn or wild rose

keyMyths.title: The Strigoi of Ploiești
keyMyths.description: A story tells of a man from Ploiești who died and returned as a Strigoi, terrorizing his village. The villagers, led by a priest, exhumed his body, staked it, and burned the heart to prevent further attacks. This tale highlights the community's fear and the drastic measures taken to combat the undead.

keyMyths.title: The Unbaptized Child
keyMyths.description: Another common belief is that unbaptized children are vulnerable to becoming Strigoi after death. To prevent this, families take great care to ensure that infants are baptized as soon as possible. This belief underscores the importance of religious rituals in protecting against evil spirits.

keyMyths.title: The Strigoi and the Eclipse
keyMyths.description: During a lunar eclipse, it is said that Strigoi become more powerful and active. Villagers would bang pots and pans and make loud noises to scare them away, believing that these actions would protect them from the Strigoi's influence during this vulnerable time. This myth connects the Strigoi to celestial events and amplifies their perceived threat.

sources.author: Eliade, Mircea
sources.work: Zalmoxis, The Vanishing God: Comparative Studies in the Religions and Folklore of Dacia and Eastern Europe
sources.description: Provides historical and religious context for understanding Romanian folklore, including the Strigoi.
sources.text: Provides historical and religious context for understanding Romanian folklore, including the Strigoi.

sources.author: Gilmore, David D.
sources.work: Monsters: Evil Beings, Mythical Beasts, and All Manner of Imaginary Terrors
sources.description: An anthropological examination of monsters across cultures, including the Strigoi and its place in Romanian society.
sources.text: An anthropological examination of monsters across cultures, including the Strigoi and its place in Romanian society.

sources.author: Barber, Paul
sources.work: Vampires, Burial, and Death: Folklore and Reality
sources.description: Explores the historical roots of vampire beliefs and their connection to burial practices and fears of the dead returning, offering insights into the Strigoi phenomenon.
sources.text: Explores the historical roots of vampire beliefs and their connection to burial practices and fears of the dead returning, offering insights into the Strigoi phenomenon.
